query: Which ISO in the US has had the highest percentage change in congestion costs from 2017 to 2024? Explain justification for the changes for that ISO
final_answer:
The ISO with the **largest percentage change in congestion costs from 2017 to 2024 was CAISO**.

### Calculation
Using the congestion-cost table from Grid Strategies’ 2024 congestion report:

- **CAISO 2017 congestion cost:** **$138 million**  
- **CAISO 2024 congestion cost:** **$734 million**  
- **Percentage change:** \((734 - 138) / 138 \times 100 = 432.6\%\)

So, **CAISO increased by about 433%**, which is the highest among the U.S. ISOs shown in the source table.  
**Source:** Grid Strategies LLC, *Transmission Congestion for 2024*, Table B1 (CAISO 2017 = $138M; 2024 = $734M). https://gridstrategiesllc.com/wp-content/uploads/GS_Transmission-Congestion-for-2024.pdf

### Comparison with other ISOs in that same table
- ERCOT: 976 → 1,900 = **+94.7%**
- ISO-NE: 41 → 37 = **-9.8%**
- MISO: 1,518 → 1,800 = **+18.6%**
- NYISO: 529 → 306 = **-42.1%**
- PJM: 698 → 1,754 = **+151.3%**
- SPP: 500 → 1,800 = **+260.0%**

**Source:** Grid Strategies LLC, *Transmission Congestion for 2024*, Table B1. https://gridstrategiesllc.com/wp-content/uploads/GS_Transmission-Congestion-for-2024.pdf

## Why CAISO changed so much
The increase is consistent with several structural and operational drivers documented by CAISO and Grid Strategies:

1. **Congestion is fundamentally driven by limited transmission capacity**
   - Grid Strategies says congestion occurs when the grid cannot deliver the lowest-cost generation to load, and that rising congestion reflects **limited transmission expansion**, **rising electricity demand**, and **extreme weather**.  
   **Source:** Grid Strategies LLC, *Transmission Congestion for 2024*. https://gridstrategiesllc.com/wp-content/uploads/GS_Transmission-Congestion-for-2024.pdf

2. **CAISO’s 2024 congestion was affected by major intertie and solar-hour flow patterns**
   - CAISO’s 2024 Market Monitoring report says **day-ahead market congestion rent was $537 million in 2024** and that **intertie congestion rent rose to $134 million**, mainly over the **Malin intertie** during the **extreme cold weather event in the Pacific Northwest in January 2024**.  
   **Source:** CAISO Department of Market Monitoring, *2024 Annual Report on Market Issues and Performance* (Executive summary / congestion section). https://www.caiso.com/documents/2024-annual-report-on-market-issues-and-performance-aug-07-2025.pdf
   - CAISO also says **prices in the Pacific Northwest were higher than elsewhere because of extreme cold weather in January and south-to-north congestion during solar hours throughout much of the year**.  
   **Source:** CAISO DMM, 2024 Annual Report / stakeholder call summary. https://www.caiso.com/notices/department-of-market-monitoring-2024-annual-report-on-market-issues-and-performance-call-on-8-12-25

3. **CAISO’s congestion pattern reflects a shift from local south-of-grid constraints to broader solar/export and intertie constraints**
   - CAISO’s 2017 report said congestion was concentrated in the **southern portion of the system**, especially **Path 26**, and that it increased prices in **San Diego Gas & Electric** and **Southern California Edison** areas.  
   **Source:** CAISO DMM, *2017 Annual Report on Market Issues and Performance*. https://www.caiso.com/documents/2017annualreportonmarketissuesandperformance.pdf
   - By 2024, CAISO’s report shows congestion remained material, but the major story included **internal congestion in the real-time market, intertie congestion, and solar-hour north/south flows** rather than just the older Path 26 pattern.  
   **Source:** CAISO DMM, *2024 Annual Report on Market Issues and Performance*. https://www.caiso.com/documents/2024-annual-report-on-market-issues-and-performance-aug-07-2025.pdf

4. **More renewable generation and solar-hour export patterns increase congestion pressure**
   - CAISO’s 2024 report notes that WEIM and CAISO transfers were **largely from the CAISO balancing area to other WEIM regions during solar hours**, while non-solar-hour transfers reversed direction or fell.  
   **Source:** CAISO DMM, 2024 Annual Report stakeholder summary. https://www.caiso.com/notices/department-of-market-monitoring-2024-annual-report-on-market-issues-and-performance-call-on-8-12-25
   - CAISO’s curtailment report also explains that **economic local curtailment** and **exceptional dispatch** are used when transmission cannot deliver least-cost energy, which is the operational symptom of congestion.  
   **Source:** CAISO, *Wind and Solar Curtailment December 31, 2024*. https://www.caiso.com/documents/wind-solar-real-time-dispatch-curtailment-report-dec-31-2024.pdf

### Bottom line
**CAISO had the highest percentage increase in congestion costs from 2017 to 2024, rising about 433%.** The main justification is a combination of **binding transmission constraints**, **higher renewable-driven flow shifts during solar hours**, and **extreme-weather/intertie stress**, especially around the **Malin intertie** in 2024.  
**Sources:** Grid Strategies 2024 congestion report; CAISO 2017 and 2024 Market Monitoring annual reports; CAISO 2024 stakeholder summary.

steps.content:
The ISO with the **largest percentage change in congestion costs from 2017 to 2024 was CAISO**.

### Calculation
Using the congestion-cost table from Grid Strategies’ 2024 congestion report:

- **CAISO 2017 congestion cost:** **$138 million**  
- **CAISO 2024 congestion cost:** **$734 million**  
- **Percentage change:** \((734 - 138) / 138 \times 100 = 432.6\%\)

So, **CAISO increased by about 433%**, which is the highest among the U.S. ISOs shown in the source table.  
**Source:** Grid Strategies LLC, *Transmission Congestion for 2024*, Table B1 (CAISO 2017 = $138M; 2024 = $734M). https://gridstrategiesllc.com/wp-content/uploads/GS_Transmission-Congestion-for-2024.pdf

### Comparison with other ISOs in that same table
- ERCOT: 976 → 1,900 = **+94.7%**
- ISO-NE: 41 → 37 = **-9.8%**
- MISO: 1,518 → 1,800 = **+18.6%**
- NYISO: 529 → 306 = **-42.1%**
- PJM: 698 → 1,754 = **+151.3%**
- SPP: 500 → 1,800 = **+260.0%**

**Source:** Grid Strategies LLC, *Transmission Congestion for 2024*, Table B1. https://gridstrategiesllc.com/wp-content/uploads/GS_Transmission-Congestion-for-2024.pdf

## Why CAISO changed so much
The increase is consistent with several structural and operational drivers documented by CAISO and Grid Strategies:

1. **Congestion is fundamentally driven by limited transmission capacity**
   - Grid Strategies says congestion occurs when the grid cannot deliver the lowest-cost generation to load, and that rising congestion reflects **limited transmission expansion**, **rising electricity demand**, and **extreme weather**.  
   **Source:** Grid Strategies LLC, *Transmission Congestion for 2024*. https://gridstrategiesllc.com/wp-content/uploads/GS_Transmission-Congestion-for-2024.pdf

2. **CAISO’s 2024 congestion was affected by major intertie and solar-hour flow patterns**
   - CAISO’s 2024 Market Monitoring report says **day-ahead market congestion rent was $537 million in 2024** and that **intertie congestion rent rose to $134 million**, mainly over the **Malin intertie** during the **extreme cold weather event in the Pacific Northwest in January 2024**.  
   **Source:** CAISO Department of Market Monitoring, *2024 Annual Report on Market Issues and Performance* (Executive summary / congestion section). https://www.caiso.com/documents/2024-annual-report-on-market-issues-and-performance-aug-07-2025.pdf
   - CAISO also says **prices in the Pacific Northwest were higher than elsewhere because of extreme cold weather in January and south-to-north congestion during solar hours throughout much of the year**.  
   **Source:** CAISO DMM, 2024 Annual Report / stakeholder call summary. https://www.caiso.com/notices/department-of-market-monitoring-2024-annual-report-on-market-issues-and-performance-call-on-8-12-25

3. **CAISO’s congestion pattern reflects a shift from local south-of-grid constraints to broader solar/export and intertie constraints**
   - CAISO’s 2017 report said congestion was concentrated in the **southern portion of the system**, especially **Path 26**, and that it increased prices in **San Diego Gas & Electric** and **Southern California Edison** areas.  
   **Source:** CAISO DMM, *2017 Annual Report on Market Issues and Performance*. https://www.caiso.com/documents/2017annualreportonmarketissuesandperformance.pdf
   - By 2024, CAISO’s report shows congestion remained material, but the major story included **internal congestion in the real-time market, intertie congestion, and solar-hour north/south flows** rather than just the older Path 26 pattern.  
   **Source:** CAISO DMM, *2024 Annual Report on Market Issues and Performance*. https://www.caiso.com/documents/2024-annual-report-on-market-issues-and-performance-aug-07-2025.pdf

4. **More renewable generation and solar-hour export patterns increase congestion pressure**
   - CAISO’s 2024 report notes that WEIM and CAISO transfers were **largely from the CAISO balancing area to other WEIM regions during solar hours**, while non-solar-hour transfers reversed direction or fell.  
   **Source:** CAISO DMM, 2024 Annual Report stakeholder summary. https://www.caiso.com/notices/department-of-market-monitoring-2024-annual-report-on-market-issues-and-performance-call-on-8-12-25
   - CAISO’s curtailment report also explains that **economic local curtailment** and **exceptional dispatch** are used when transmission cannot deliver least-cost energy, which is the operational symptom of congestion.  
   **Source:** CAISO, *Wind and Solar Curtailment December 31, 2024*. https://www.caiso.com/documents/wind-solar-real-time-dispatch-curtailment-report-dec-31-2024.pdf

### Bottom line
**CAISO had the highest percentage increase in congestion costs from 2017 to 2024, rising about 433%.** The main justification is a combination of **binding transmission constraints**, **higher renewable-driven flow shifts during solar hours**, and **extreme-weather/intertie stress**, especially around the **Malin intertie** in 2024.  
**Sources:** Grid Strategies 2024 congestion report; CAISO 2017 and 2024 Market Monitoring annual reports; CAISO 2024 stakeholder summary.
